quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Sample proportion (p̂)"?

options.text: A phenomenon where an association between two variables reverses direction when data are combined across a lurking variable
Randomly selecting entire groups (clusters) and surveying all individuals within chosen clusters; practical when a population list is unavailable
The fraction of successes in a sample: p̂ = X/n, where X is the number of successes
For Y = a + bX: E(Y) = a + b·E(X) and Var(Y) = b²·Var(X); adding a constant shifts the mean but does not change spread

quiz_questions.explanation: "Sample proportion (p̂)" is defined as: The fraction of successes in a sample: p̂ = X/n, where X is the number of successes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mean of p̂"?

options.text: V = √(χ²/(n·min(r-1, c-1))); ranges from 0 to 1, measuring the strength of association between two categorical variables
A study in which the researcher observes and records data without imposing a treatment or intervention on the subjects
μp̂ = p; the sample proportion is an unbiased estimator of the population proportion
One-sided: Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀ (p-value is area in one tail); Two-sided: Hₐ: p ≠ p₀ (p-value is area in both tails)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mean of p̂" is defined as: μp̂ = p; the sample proportion is an unbiased estimator of the population proportion. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Standard deviation of p̂"?

options.text: The t-value with area C in the middle of the t-distribution with df = n-1; found from t-table or technology for the desired confidence level
An unobserved variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a false impression of a direct relationship
σp̂ = √(p(1-p)/n); decreases as n increases, meaning larger samples give more precise estimates
t-procedures are robust to moderate non-normality for large samples but can be misleading for small samples from highly skewed distributions

quiz_questions.explanation: "Standard deviation of p̂" is defined as: σp̂ = √(p(1-p)/n); decreases as n increases, meaning larger samples give more precise estimates. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Normal approximation conditions"?

options.text: The probability of obtaining a test statistic as extreme as or more extreme than the observed value, assuming H₀ is true
Variance addition rules require independence; for dependent variables, covariance terms must be included
The sampling distribution of p̂ is approximately normal when np ≥ 10 AND n(1-p) ≥ 10 (Large Counts condition)
The proportion of variation in y explained by the linear relationship with x; ranges from 0 to 1

quiz_questions.explanation: "Normal approximation conditions" is defined as: The sampling distribution of p̂ is approximately normal when np ≥ 10 AND n(1-p) ≥ 10 (Large Counts condition). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "10% condition"?

options.text: When sampling without replacement, n should be less than 10% of the population to treat observations as approximately independent
A scatterplot of residuals versus the explanatory variable; used to check whether a linear model is appropriate for the data
As the number of trials increases, the sample proportion of successes converges toward the true probability
With replacement: draws are independent; without replacement: draws are dependent because the population changes after each draw

quiz_questions.explanation: "10% condition" is defined as: When sampling without replacement, n should be less than 10% of the population to treat observations as approximately independent. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The fraction of successes in a sample: p̂ = X/n, where X is the number of successes"?

options.text: Null hypothesis for slope
Reading regression output
Sample proportion (p̂)
Range

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Sample proportion (p̂)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "μp̂ = p; the sample proportion is an unbiased estimator of the population proportion"?

options.text: Explanatory variable
Quantitative variable
Mean of p̂
Multiplication rule for independents

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mean of p̂".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "σp̂ = √(p(1-p)/n); decreases as n increases, meaning larger samples give more precise estimates"?

options.text: Large Counts condition (two samples)
Residual plot
Standard deviation of p̂
Standard normal table (z-table)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Standard deviation of p̂".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The sampling distribution of p̂ is approximately normal when np ≥ 10 AND n(1-p) ≥ 10 (Large Counts condition)"?

options.text: Mean difference (d̄)
Normal approximation conditions
Mutually exclusive events
Coefficient of determination (r²)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Normal approximation conditions".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "When sampling without replacement, n should be less than 10% of the population to treat observations as approximately independent"?

options.text: 10% condition
Confidence interval for p
Sampling distribution
Margin of error

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "10% condition".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions is accurate?

options.text: Experiments use random assignment to eliminate confounding, allowing causal conclusions.
A randomized block design combines the benefits of both techniques: blocking controls for known confounders while randomization balances unknown confounders across treatment groups.
The F-test from ANOVA output provides the same conclusion for simple linear regression (F = t²).
Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions describes the distribution of p̂ = X/n across all possible samples, where X is the count of successes in n independent trials.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions?

options.text: Confidence Intervals for Slopes estimate the true population slope β₁ using b₁ ± t*·SE(b₁) with df = n-2.
The sampling distribution of p̂ has mean p and standard deviation √(p(1-p)/n), and by the CLT is approximately normal when np ≥ 10 and n(1-p) ≥ 10.
For independent random variables: Var(X ± Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y)—variances always add, even for differences.
Proper identification of variable types determines which statistical methods, graphs, and summary measures are appropriate.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions?

options.text: Comparing proportions is essential in clinical trials, A/B testing, and any study comparing success rates between groups.
Paired designs are more powerful than independent two-sample designs because they eliminate subject-to-subject variability, isolating the treatment effect.
This distribution forms the basis for inference about population proportions using z-procedures.
Comparing conditional distributions across categories reveals whether an association exists between two categorical variables.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions?

options.text: Controlling includes keeping conditions identical across groups, standardizing procedures, and using control groups as baselines for comparison.
Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions describes the distribution of p̂ = X/n across all possible samples, where X is the count of successes in n independent trials.
Binomial and Geometric Distributions model two important types of count data.
The test statistic χ² = Σ(observed - expected)²/expected measures overall departure from the expected distribution, with larger values indicating worse fit.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions?

options.text: t-Distributions replace the normal distribution when the population standard deviation σ is unknown and must be estimated by the sample standard deviation s.
The sampling distribution of p̂ has mean p and standard deviation √(p(1-p)/n), and by the CLT is approximately normal when np ≥ 10 and n(1-p) ≥ 10.

Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that regardless of the population's shape, the sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size n increases (generally n ≥ 30 is sufficient).
The choice of transformation depends on the curvature pattern, and predictions must be back-transformed to the original scale.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions?

options.text: Pre-election polls using the sampling distribution of p̂ to calculate the margin of error for estimating a candidate's true support level
Environmental agency testing whether mean mercury concentration in fish exceeds the 0.5 ppm safety limit (H₀: μ = 0.5, Hₐ: μ > 0.5) to issue consumption advisories
College admissions comparing applicants who took different standardized tests (SAT vs. ACT) by converting to common z-score scale
Linearizing the relationship between time and radioactive decay to estimate half-lives using transformed regression

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions?

options.text: Astronomy: the power relationship between a planet's orbital period and distance from the sun (Kepler's Third Law: T² ∝ r³) linearized using log-log transformation
Pharmaceutical trials determining sample size needed so the sampling distribution of the cure rate is narrow enough to detect a clinically meaningful effect
Economists checking LINE conditions before publishing a regression model of how education level predicts income
Modeling population growth of bacteria over time using exponential transformation, then predicting colony size at future time points

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: A manufacturer finding a significant chi-square result for defect type vs. production shift, then identifying that the night shift drives most of the association
Demonstrating the CLT with simulations: rolling dice (uniform distribution) and plotting the mean of 30 rolls—the distribution of means is approximately normal despite the uniform population
Social media analysis estimating the proportion of posts containing misinformation and computing a confidence interval using the normal approximation to p̂
Laboratory experiments controlling temperature, humidity, and light exposure when testing plant growth under different fertilizer treatments

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions?

options.text: Pre-election polls using the sampling distribution of p̂ to calculate the margin of error for estimating a candidate's true support level
Environmental agency testing whether mean mercury concentration in fish exceeds the 0.5 ppm safety limit (H₀: μ = 0.5, Hₐ: μ > 0.5) to issue consumption advisories
Modeling population growth of bacteria over time using exponential transformation, then predicting colony size at future time points
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions in practice?

options.text: Pharmaceutical trials determining sample size needed so the sampling distribution of the cure rate is narrow enough to detect a clinically meaningful effect
Testing whether a coin is fair (H₀: p = 0.5) by flipping it 100 times and computing the z-statistic from the observed proportion of heads
News organizations reporting poll results with margin of error ('52% favor the measure ± 3 percentage points') derived from the confidence interval formula
Astronomy: the power relationship between a planet's orbital period and distance from the sun (Kepler's Third Law: T² ∝ r³) linearized using log-log transformation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions.
